data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Harness can strictly beat self-consistency only at k >= 7 with base error in [0.15, 0.35] and critic precision exceeding a sqrt(k)-scaled threshold because at low k the plurality is too coarse for critic filtering to improve without also breaking fragile correct majorities.", "mechanism": "At k=5, the plurality margin is quantized in steps of 1/5 = 0.2. A critic flag that removes one candidate from a 3-2 correct majority creates a 2-2 tie (broken), and a critic flag that removes one candidate from a 3-2 wrong majority creates a 2-2 tie (not fixed, just neutralized). So at k=5, filtering has a high break-to-fix ratio because removal often produces ties rather than plurality flips. At k=7 or k=9, the finer granularity means a single flag can flip a 4-3 majority without creating a tie, and the critic can target individual wrong candidates more surgically. The measured k=5 data showing zero net gain is consistent with this granularity constraint. Strict harness advantage requires k >= 7, base error where 3-4 way splits are common (e ~ 0.2-0.3), and critic precision above ~0.5 * sqrt(5/k) to compensate for the residual break risk.", "scope": {"covers": ["k in {7, 9, 11}", "base error in [0.15, 0.35]", "critic precision > 0.5 * sqrt(5/k) with recall > 0.4", "comparison against k=5 as control"], "excludes": ["k=5: granularity too coarse for filtering to flip without breaking", "base error < 0.15: insufficient wrong-plurality questions to fix", "base error > 0.35: wrong pluralities dominate, SC fails regardless"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "k=7 and k=9 harness-minus-SC advantage is <= k=5 harness-minus-SC advantage at matched base error and critic precision", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "The tent-shaped gain curve predicted as a function of base error does not appear at k=7, i.e., gain is flat or monotonically decreasing across [0.15, 0.35]", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "At k=9 and base error ~0.25, harness accuracy equals SC accuracy within 2 percentage points despite critic precision > 0.5", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}], "prose_notes": "This is the most atypical candidate because it predicts that k=5 (the only measured value) is structurally incapable of showing harness advantage, making the measured ties expected rather than surprising. The decisive experiment varies k across {5, 7, 9} at matched base error (~0.20) with the same critic, testing whether granularity is the binding constraint. If harness gain emerges at k=7 but not k=5 with the same precision/recall, the granularity mechanism is confirmed."}
